macro_segments.title: The Physical Structure of the Tanur (Oven) — Box Shape versus V-Shape

micro_segments.title: Opening inquiry into the boat-like (V-shaped) description from the Baraita
Reconciling the Baraita's description of a beehive-like vessel with a squared opening
Tosafot's interpretation: the oven combined a V-shape at the bottom with vertical walls rising from the top
Second Baraita supporting the boat-shaped theory with four golden sideboards that spread like palm fronds
Debate over the meaning of merosheihem (at their heads) and the function of the spreading sideboards
Rambam's ruling that the box-shaped interpretation is superior and best reconciles the sources

macro_segments.title: Lechem HaPanim Disqualification During Journeys (Masa'ot) — The Debate of Rebbi Yochanan and Rebbi Yehoshua ben Levi

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the question: does Lechem HaPanim become pasul when camps travel?
First opinion: bread becomes pasul because there is no longer an Ohel Moed and the bread exits it
Second opinion: bread does not become pasul because the Torah commands it constantly remain on the Shulchan
Reframing the debate: the bread on the Shulchan does not become pasul, but bread removed from the Shulchan does
Logical discussion of how bread remains stable on the Shulchan and how it is transported during journeys

macro_segments.title: Kodashim Disqualification During Journeys — The Dispute of Rabbi Eliezer and the Sages

micro_segments.title: Baraita from Zevachim: when camps move, kodashim become pasul beyotzei, but zavim and metzora'im must still be sent out
The contradiction: if kodashim become pasul during travel, why is Lechem HaPanim an exception?
Resolution: bread on the Shulchan is kosher; bread off the Shulchan is treated like other kodashei kodashim and becomes pasul
Inference from the debate: camps must have traveled at night, when bread off the table is still potentially kosher but no longer in the Mikdash context
Rabbi Eliezer's view: when Ohel Moed has no sacred status (no Machaneh Shechina), zavim and metzora'im are not sent out
The three levels of purity restrictions: tamei meis (prohibited only from Machaneh Shechina), zav (prohibited from Machaneh Leviyah), metzora (prohibited from all three camps)

macro_segments.title: Preparation of Lechem HaPanim and Shtei HaLechem — Location and Method

micro_segments.title: First opinion: kneading and shaping done outside the Mikdash; baking done inside with the sanctified oven
The problem: if baking in a sanctified vessel causes kedusha, the bread becomes pasul belina if baked before Shabbat
Strong challenge (Rav Ashi's attempt and rejection): proposing that bemakom zerizim (with zealous overseers) could allow flexibility in location
Gemara's refutation: if zealous overseers are needed during baking, they should also be needed during kneading
Rabbi Yehuda's position: all preparation done inside the Mikdash (dry measures were sanctified)
Rabbi Shimon's position: all preparation could be done outside; sanctification happens only when placed on the Shulchan or with the sacrifice

macro_segments.title: Derech Chol Interpretation — David's Request for Lechem HaPanim at Nov

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Abbahu: both Rabbi Yehuda and Rabbi Shimon derive their opinions from the same verse about derech chol
The Biblical narrative: David asks for bread at Nov, the priest explains only Lechem HaPanim is available if David's men are pure
Rav Yehuda's reading: David insists the bread was already sanctified in holy vessels, thus it must have been made on a weekday (not Shabbat)
Rabbi Shimon's reading: David says the bread should have been made on a weekday using non-sanctified vessels, avoiding Shabbat violation
Gemara clarification: the verses address me'ila (misappropriation) issues, not the sanctification method
David's final ruling: even bread with full sanctification status can be given due to pikuach nefesh (danger to life)
